title: Visual accessibility audit

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Visual accessibility audit; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Visual accessibility audit
sources.claim: Accessible CSS preserves visible focus, zoom and reflow, contrast, target size, reading order, user preferences, and content visibility.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Accessible CSS preserves visible focus, zoom and reflow, contrast, target size, reading order, user preferences, and content visibility.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: At 200 percent zoom a form reflows without two-dimensional scrolling and focus remains visible. Your goal is to record and repair findings from a WCAG-informed visual and interaction audit. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Responsive design and motion; Stylesheet organization; Color and contrast; Reduced motion and input preferences; Overflow and containment. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Accessible CSS preserves visible focus, zoom and reflow, contrast, target size, reading order, user preferences, and content visibility. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Visual hiding methods differ, generated content may be unavailable, and reordering can separate visual from DOM navigation. This draft therefore rejects: Accessibility is handled entirely by semantic HTML, so CSS cannot create barriers.

includes: Review contrast, focus visibility, reflow, zoom, text spacing, target size and motion preferences.
Record and repair findings from a WCAG-informed visual and interaction audit.
excludes: Visual hiding methods differ, generated content may be unavailable, and reordering can separate visual from DOM navigation.

outcomes: Record and repair findings from a WCAG-informed visual and interaction audit.

examples.title: Visual accessibility audit: concrete case
examples.situation: At 200 percent zoom a form reflows without two-dimensional scrolling and focus remains visible.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: At 200 percent zoom a form reflows without two-dimensional scrolling and focus remains visible.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Test CSS-driven focus, contrast, reflow, order, motion, and visibility because presentation can create or remove access barriers.
examples.result: The result follows because Test CSS-driven focus, contrast, reflow, order, motion, and visibility because presentation can create or remove access barriers.

examples.title: Visual accessibility audit: boundary case
examples.situation: Visual hiding methods differ, generated content may be unavailable, and reordering can separate visual from DOM navigation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Visual hiding methods differ, generated content may be unavailable, and reordering can separate visual from DOM navigation.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Accessibility is handled entirely by semantic HTML, so CSS cannot create barriers.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Test CSS-driven focus, contrast, reflow, order, motion, and visibility because presentation can create or remove access barriers.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Accessibility is handled entirely by semantic HTML, so CSS cannot create barriers.
misconceptions.correction: Test CSS-driven focus, contrast, reflow, order, motion, and visibility because presentation can create or remove access barriers.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: Accessibility is handled entirely by semantic HTML, so CSS cannot create barriers.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Visual accessibility audit, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Visual accessibility audit before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.

recap.summary: You should now be able to record and repair findings from a WCAG-informed visual and interaction audit. Keep this boundary in view: Visual hiding methods differ, generated content may be unavailable, and reordering can separate visual from DOM navigation.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Visual accessibility audit using exact CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling terms.
Why is this claim incorrect: Accessibility is handled entirely by semantic HTML, so CSS cannot create barriers.
Apply checkpoint 'presentation can create or remove access barriers' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Responsive site design system”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
